calculated_metrics.confidence_note: Cost per outcome calculation is not available. This organisation conducts significant multi-domain work (e.g., human education, environmental conservation). Dividing the total budget solely by quantified animal outcomes would artificially inflate the cost and misrepresent their financial efficiency.

details.formula: Calculation aborted due to multi-domain operations.

calculated_metrics.name: Cost Per Outcome (USD)

The group was tasked with drafting and leading global consultations which led to the 2021 passage of a new International Maritime Organization global document, Guidelines for the Prevention and Suppression of the Smuggling of Wildlife on Ships Engaged in International Maritime Traffic.", "Highest tractability intervention found: 'Policy And Legal Advocacy'", "EA Rationale: Policy and legal advocacy can create systemic, legally-binding protections for animals, representing a highly tractable path to impact."]
details.formula: Mapping of charity's intervention types against the EA Intervention Tractability evidence base.

details.formula: Highest level of self-reported evidence cited in impact metrics

Proportional analysis of beneficiary populations (farmed/wild vs. companion)

beneficiaries.location: Mai Po Nature Reserve, Hong Kong

metrics.context_qualifier: Reduction achieved by 6 companies adopting LOOP+ and 39 manufacturers participating in LCMP programs in FY25, compared to base year levels.

counterfactual_baseline.description: Emissions if companies continued business as usual or base year levels without intervention.

metrics.evidence_quote: Collectively, these efforts led to a 41% reduction (256,520 tonnes of CO2e) from business-as-usual scenarios, and a 48% reduction (348,180 tonnes of CO2e) compared to base year levels.

metrics.context_qualifier: Mai Po Nature Reserve, a Ramsar-designated wetland, supports over 80,000 migratory birds annually.
metrics.evidence_quote: The organization manages the Mai Po Nature Reserve, a Ramsar-designated wetland since 1995 that supports over 80,000 migratory birds and more than 2,050 recorded species, in partnership with Hong Kong's Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation Department since 1983.
metrics.metric_name: Migratory Birds Supported by Mai Po Nature Reserve

metrics.context_qualifier: Identified from over 7.3 million cargo bookings screened by innovative technology solution to combat illegal wildlife trade.
metrics.evidence_quote: screened more than 7.3 million cargo bookings and identified over 25,000 high-risk shipments - a major advancement in combating IWT in maritime supply chains.

metrics.context_qualifier: Global population of Black-faced Spoonbill, a ten-fold increase from 1988-1990 low of 288 individuals, recognized by BirdLife International.
metrics.evidence_quote: The award recognizes WWF and other conservation organizations' efforts and achievements in protecting the endangered Black-faced spoonbill. The latest census has revealed a global population of over 2,700 birds, almost a ten-fold increase from a low of 288 individuals recorded in 1988-1990.

metrics.context_qualifier: Benefited from nutritious eco-fish meals, promoting sustainable consumption and food equity through Powering Our Wetlands project.
metrics.evidence_quote: 2,200+ underprivileged community members benefited from nutritious eco-fish meals, promoting sustainable consumption and food equity.
metrics.metric_name: Underprivileged Community Members Benefited from Eco-fish Meals

metrics.context_qualifier: Nurtured in nursery and outplanted to Tolo Harbour and Channel, with an 85% survival rate.
metrics.evidence_quote: So far, Reviving Our Corals has nurtured 680 coral fragments in the Hoi Ha Marine Life Centre nursery. These have been successfully outplanted to sites in the Tolo Harbour and Channel, and 85% have survived.

significant_events.event_name: Passage of IMO Guidelines for Wildlife Smuggling Prevention
significant_events.source_quote: In September 2020, WWF-Hong Kong helped create a working group to combat wildlife smuggling. The group was tasked with drafting and leading global consultations which led to the 2021 passage of a new International Maritime Organization global document, Guidelines for the Prevention and Suppression of the Smuggling of Wildlife on Ships Engaged in International Maritime Traffic.
significant_events.summary: WWF-HK helped create a working group that led to the 2021 passage of new IMO global guidelines to combat wildlife smuggling on ships.

significant_events.event_name: Global Shipping Companies Banned Shark Fin Shipments
significant_events.source_quote: 16 of the top 20 global shipping companies, over 60% of the global shipping market, banned shark fin shipments.
significant_events.summary: 16 of the top 20 global shipping companies (over 60% of market) banned shark fin shipments, a WWF-HK achievement.

significant_events.event_name: Contribution to 2021 Prohibition on Elephant Ivory Sales
significant_events.source_quote: Key achievements include advocating for a 2013 ban on trawling in Hong Kong waters to protect marine ecosystems, contributing to the 2021 prohibition on elephant ivory sales with severe penalties for violations, and supporting the creation of early marine parks such as Hoi Ha Wan in 1996.
significant_events.summary: Contributed to the 2021 prohibition on elephant ivory sales in Hong Kong with severe penalties for violations.

significant_events.event_name: Advocacy for 2013 Ban on Trawling in Hong Kong
significant_events.source_quote: Key achievements include advocating for a 2013 ban on trawling in Hong Kong waters to protect marine ecosystems, contributing to the 2021 prohibition on elephant ivory sales with severe penalties for violations, and supporting the creation of early marine parks such as Hoi Ha Wan in 1996.
significant_events.summary: Advocated for and contributed to the 2013 ban on trawling in Hong Kong waters to protect marine ecosystems.

significant_events.event_name: Launch of WWF Jockey Club Smart Wetland Management Project
significant_events.source_quote: Thanks to a substantial grant from The Hong Kong Jockey Club Charities Trust, WWF-Hong Kong launched the WWF Jockey Club Smart Wetland Management Project ("The project") in 2025. Building on successful trials of smart solutions conducted in Mai Po in the past, this three-year initiative will be Hong Kong's first long-term effort to apply smart technology to wetland management in a holistic, systematic way.
significant_events.summary: Three-year initiative launched in 2025 to apply smart technology holistically to wetland management in Mai Po, enhancing hydrological control and monitoring.

significant_events.event_name: Release of The State of Hong Kong Biodiversity 2025 Report
significant_events.source_quote: The 2025 fiscal year saw several landmark accomplishments – the release of The State of Hong Kong Biodiversity 2025 report, a collaboration with the Hong Kong Institute of Landscape Architects (HKILA) to promote Nature-based Solutions (NbS) in urban development, and community-led advocacy initiatives to preserve critical agricultural and wetland habitats.
significant_events.summary: Published a comprehensive report on Hong Kong's biodiversity, revealing 21 species disappeared and over a quarter at risk, advocating for policy changes.

significant_events.event_name: Mai Po Nature Reserve received Gold Star Wetland Centre Award
significant_events.source_quote: On 27 July 2025, Mai Po Nature Reserve received the prestigious Gold Star Wetland Centre Award from Wetland Link International, joining just 15 other sites globally and becoming one of only three Gold Star centres worldwide.
significant_events.summary: Mai Po Nature Reserve received prestigious Gold Star Wetland Centre Award from Wetland Link International, recognizing excellence in wetland education and conservation.

significant_events.event_name: Launch of Lee Hysan Foundation 'Growing Mai Po' Project
significant_events.source_quote: Recognising the urgent need to preserve these irreplaceable natural and cultural landscapes, we launched the three-year Lee Hysan Foundation "Growing Mai Po" (GMP) project in June 2024, supported by the Lee Hysan Foundation.
significant_events.summary: Three-year project launched in June 2024 to restore rice paddies and enhance wetland habitats for migratory birds using smart technologies.

significant_events.source_quote: In July 2024, we launched the two-year "Smart Wetland 2.0" project, funded by the Countryside Conservation Fund Scheme under the Countryside Conservation Office. The project aims to upgrade Mai Po Nature Reserve's mammal monitoring system by integrating the cutting-edge technology to enhance research and monitoring efficiency.
significant_events.summary: Two-year project launched in July 2024 to upgrade Mai Po's mammal monitoring system using smart camera traps and AI for efficiency.

significant_events.event_name: Launch of East Asia's First 'Seafood Guide'
significant_events.source_quote: Launched East Asia's first “Seafood Guide” to promote sustainable seafood and raise awareness of marine biodiversity.
significant_events.summary: Launched East Asia's first 'Seafood Guide' to promote sustainable seafood and raise awareness of marine biodiversity.

data.original_text: From our work managing Mai Po Nature Reserve and conserving iconic species, we have expanded our mission to better serve Hong Kong's conservation and education needs.

WWF-Hong Kong has been working since 1981 to deliver solutions for a living planet through conservation, footprint and education programmes, with the aim of transforming Hong Kong into Asia's most sustainable city.

WWF-HK's core service is conservation. Environmental education is a tool to engage people in conservation and hence achieve our ultimate goal. WWF-HK's conservation programmes are divided into 5 pillars- Freshwater and Wetlands, Marine, Terrestrial and Conservation Policy, Oceans Sustainability and Climate Change.
Visitors are welcome at some five centres located Central 1, Tramway path, Island House Tai Po, Mai Po, and Hoi Ha Wan.
WWF-Hong Kong has been working since 1981 to deliver solutions for a living planet through Conservation, Footprint and Education programmes.
The past 40 years have seen us make many significant achievements in nature conservation, education and learning, and corporate and community engagement.
Launched East Asia's first “Seafood Guide” to promote sustainable seafood and raise awareness of marine biodiversity.
16 of the top 20 global shipping companies, over 60% of the global shipping market, banned shark fin shipments.
The Hong Kong Jockey Club Charities Trust awarded WWF-Hong Kong HK$347 million to transform Mai Po Nature Reserve into a 21st Century Nature Classroom.

The organization manages the Mai Po Nature Reserve, a Ramsar-designated wetland since 1995 that supports over 80,000 migratory birds and more than 2,050 recorded species, in partnership with Hong Kong's Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation Department since 1983.

Key achievements include advocating for a 2013 ban on trawling in Hong Kong waters to protect marine ecosystems, contributing to the 2021 prohibition on elephant ivory sales with severe penalties for violations, and supporting the creation of early marine parks such as Hoi Ha Wan in 1996.
It has also influenced legislative enhancements in 2021 to classify wildlife trafficking as organized crime, bolstering enforcement against smuggling.
WWF-Hong Kong and the Mai Po Management Committee are honored to be the first-ever Hong Kong conservation organization to receive the BirdLife International Conservation Achievement Award.

The award recognizes WWF and other conservation organizations' efforts and achievements in protecting the endangered Black-faced spoonbill. The latest census has revealed a global population of over 2,700 birds, almost a ten-fold increase from a low of 288 individuals recorded in 1988-1990.

This year, we continued our wetland conservation work by maintaining and enhancing the Mai Po wetlands to ensure they remain a biodiversity-rich habitat for wildlife and a suitable wintering ground for migratory birds.

We also worked to empower managers of other sites along the East Asian- Australasian Flyway, building regional climate resilience and helping these wetlands remain vital, vibrant places where migratory birds and other wildlife can thrive.

The 2025 fiscal year saw several landmark accomplishments – the release of The State of Hong Kong Biodiversity 2025 report, a collaboration with the Hong Kong Institute of Landscape Architects (HKILA) to promote Nature-based Solutions (NbS) in urban development, and community-led advocacy initiatives to preserve critical agricultural and wetland habitats.

We marked the first anniversary of completing the Mai Po Peter Scott Infrastructure Upgrade Project. The new Peter Scott Visitor Centre has emerged as a hub for wetland conservation, research, education, and community engagement.

In 2018, WWF-Hong Kong began establishing a strong conservation foundation at Shui Hau on Lantau Island, employing a combination of ecological surveys, public education and active community engagement. This year, we launched two initiatives, “Discover Shui Hau” and “Smart Shui Hau”, to aid in the development of a holistic management plan for the area by boosting community involvement, education and visitor management.

In February 2024, we released Adopting Nature-based Solutions for a Better Hong Kong as a visionary study, hoping to raise local awareness about NbS and explore its applications in Hong Kong and the Greater Bay Area (GBA).

The report is accompanied by the Hong Kong Terrestrial Biodiversity Hotspot Map 2025, which highlights 27 critical biodiversity hotspots outside the protected-area system. These hotspots, which cover merely 6% of Hong Kong's land area, are home to about 95% of at-risk species.

In September 2020, WWF-Hong Kong helped create a working group to combat wildlife smuggling. The group was tasked with drafting and leading global consultations which led to the 2021 passage of a new International Maritime Organization global document, Guidelines for the Prevention and Suppression of the Smuggling of Wildlife on Ships Engaged in International Maritime Traffic.

aliases: World Wide Fund for Nature Hong Kong
